title: ReGen: Hierarchical Multi-Prompt Representation Generation for Efficient Waveform Diffusion Models
abstract:
Representation alignment (REPA) has been investigated to accelerate diffusion training, but we observe that regularizing intermediate representations in diffusion Transformers (DiT) may implicitly entangle latents and limit generative capacity. 
To address this issue, we propose ReGen, a hierarchical multi-prompt representation generation framework that jointly estimates multiple vector fields for both representations and data within a single diffusion model.
We further introduce generalized flow matching (GFM) to improve the generalization of conditional flow matching (CFM). We validate ReGen on single-stage waveform diffusion models including neural audio codec and Wave-VAE. ReGen significantly improves waveform generation quality from highly compressed latent representations at 12.5 Hz. We also present ReGenVoice, a latent diffusion model (LDM)-based text-to-speech model that achieves strong speech intelligibility (WER) and speaker similarity (SIM) with a small dataset. Moreover, operating the LDM at 6.25 Hz with rich semantic and acoustic latent representation enables efficient training and sampling, requiring only 1 day of training on 4 GPUs and fast inference with an RTF of 0.08. Audio samples are available at https://regenvoice.github.io/demo/
decision_comment:
This paper proposes ReGen, a hierarchical multi-prompt representation generation framework for waveform diffusion models. It introduces a conceptual shift from representation alignment to representation generation, aiming to improve generative capacity for high-resolution audio. The authors also introduce Generalized Flow Matching with a velocity-space repulsive term to prevent trajectory collapse. The framework is evaluated on a neural audio codec, Wave-VAE, and a TTS system, demonstrating efficient inference and strong performance at low bitrates.

The core technical contributions—the ReGen framework and the GFM objective—are mathematically sound and well-received by the majority of the reviewers. The primary obstacle is Reviewer KFEY's strong objection to the baseline evaluations. However, the authors have provided a rigorous and heavily cited defense demonstrating that the variance in the CosyVoice2 baseline WER is a well-documented phenomenon in recent top-tier literature, stemming from the inherent randomness of top-p sampling and CFM-based decoders.

Because the authors have sufficiently addressed the methodological and baseline concerns, and the remaining reviewers agree on the strength and novelty of the architecture, the paper warrants inclusion in the program. I am recommending a Weak Accept, with a flag for SAC discussion due to the strong author-reviewer disagreement regarding standard speech evaluation protocols.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes ReGen, a hierarchical representation generation framework for waveform diffusion models. Instead of aligning representations (REPA), it jointly generates semantic, acoustic, and waveform representations within a single model, along with a generalized flow matching (GFM) objective. The method shows improvements in low-bitrate reconstruction and TTS.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
The paper focuses on an important challenge in low-bitrate audio generation. By introducing generation objectives at different levels in the diffusion decoding, the model is guided to achieve high-quality reconstruction from compact latent representations. Promising reconstruction performance is achieved under low-bitrate settings, even compared to higher-bitrate codecs.

Weakness: Lack of evidence on learned representations
While the paper claims improved representation learning through ReGen, the evaluation is largely limited to end-to-end reconstruction. Crucially, the paper does not provide a direct comparison between REPA-based latents and ReGen-based latents in TTS setting, nor any comparison in the semantic representation (ASR, emotion, etc.). This comparison is important to demonstrate whether the learned representations are actually improved for generative modeling.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. More evidence on the capability of learned representations in other tasks besides reconstruction are needed.
2.  λregen is not described — is it the same as λatt?
3.The descriptions in Tables 4 and 5 are somewhat confusing. It is unclear whether ReGen-H uses VAE latents or codec tokens. This is important, as it determines whether Generalized Flow Matching is also effective for ReGenVAE. What does w_regen represent in Table 5?
4.It is unclear whether Tables 1 and 2 use prompts. If prompts are used, the fairness of speaker-related metrics (e.g., SPK-SIM) may be affected.
5.In Table 2, the Nq for VAE models may be incorrect. If I understand correctly, ReGenVAE uses continuous latent representations without quantization, so this should be corrected.
reviews.final_justification: The rebuttal well addresses my concerns on ablation study in generation tasks so I raise my rating to 4 - Weak accept.

reviews.summary: ReGen proposes shifting from representation alignment (REPA) to representation generation in DiT-based waveform synthesis. Rather than regularizing intermediate representations toward SSL targets, ReGen jointly generates SSL, Mel, and waveform signals within a single hierarchical DiT using a masked multi-prompt infilling strategy. A companion contribution, Generalized Flow Matching (GFM), adds a velocity-space repulsive term to prevent stochastic trajectory collapse. The framework is validated on a neural audio codec (ReGenTokenizer), a VAE (ReGenVAE), and an LDM-based TTS system (ReGenVoice), showing gains in speech intelligibility, speaker similarity, and inference efficiency.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
## Strengths

- **Principled conceptual shift.** Moving from representation alignment (REPA) to representation generation is well motivated and directly supported by ablation results (Table 4) and Wang et al. (2025d).
- **Well-structured ablation.** Incremental component analysis in Table 4 clearly attributes gains — the SPK-SIM jump from REPA-H (0.38) to ReGen-H (0.54+) is substantial and consistently reproduced.
- **Practical system simplification.** ReGenVoice eliminates the need for separate speaker encoders, Mel decoders, and vocoders while maintaining competitive quality.
- **Data efficiency in the TTS stage.** ReGenVoice achieves WER 1.46 / SIM 0.64 with only 0.5k hours of LDM fine-tuning data, competitive with systems using 100k+ hours for TTS training (Table 6). Note that ReGenVAE, whose latents ReGenVoice operates on was itself pretrained on Emilia (100k hours); the data efficiency claim applies specifically 
to the TTS training stage, not the full pipeline.
- **Novel objective design.** The velocity-space repulsive term in GFM (Eq. 5) is a mathematically coherent and domain-appropriate adaptation of Gritsenko et al. (2020).

---

## Weaknesses

 **1. Unexplained pitch degradation in ReGenTokenizer.**
  Pitch error reported in Table 2 (94.5 Hz) is substantially worse than all baselines (DAC 22.3, EnCodec 32.1, WavTokenizer 41.1). The ablation in Table 4 reveals a progressive pitch degradation: ReGen-H + GFM achieves 57.5 Hz, which worsens to 118.1 Hz after the auxiliary decoder stage (CosyVoice2 token distillation on FSQ), and then partially recovers to 94.5 Hz after GAN post-training. The auxiliary decoder step is therefore the primary source of pitch degradation, not GAN fine-tuning alone — though the final model still ends at 94.5 Hz, far above all baselines (DAC 22.3, EnCodec 32.1, WavTokenizer 41.1). Neither step is analyzed in the paper. This regression is not discussed in the paper.

**2. Adversarial post-training is not controlled in headline comparisons.**
Tables 1–3 report ReGenTokenizer and ReGenVAE results after adversarial 
post-training, whereas all baselines (including the strongest, REPA-H) 
are evaluated without GAN fine-tuning. Table 4 does show a sequential 
ablation isolating ReGen from GAN effects within the same training 
pipeline, but it does not include a REPA-H + adversarial post-training 
row. Without this specific control, it is not possible to determine 
from the headline tables how much of the reported gain in Tables 1–3 
is attributable to the ReGen architecture versus the adversarial 
training stage that all baselines lack.

**3. Incorrect ground-truth comparison claim in §7.3.**
  The paper claims that ReGenVAE outperforms ground-truth samples. However, Table 3 shows that while WER improves (2.03 vs. 2.14), speaker similarity does not (0.72 vs. 0.73). The claim therefore holds only for WER but not for SIM and should be clarified.


### Minor

1. ReGen-U collapses at λ_regen = 0.1 (WER 89.94) and partially recovers when the weight is reduced to 0.01. The paper does not explain whether this instability arises from shared-layer gradient interference or simple weight scaling, which weakens the justification for the hierarchical design.

2. ReGenVAE (continuous latent space) and ReGenTokenizer (discrete FSQ quantization) appear in the same evaluation tables without emphasizing that continuous VAEs represent an inherently easier reconstruction regime than discrete codecs. This may lead readers to misinterpret the performance differences.

3. The manuscript header states "Preliminary work" despite presenting a full experimental evaluation. This label should be removed or clarified.

4. GFM is presented as a general improvement to conditional flow matching, yet all evaluations are conducted on audio tasks. Even a simple non-audio toy experiment would strengthen this claim.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
**Q1.** Table 4 shows pitch error progressing from 57.5 Hz (ReGen-H + GFM) to 118.1 Hz after auxiliary decoder addition, then recovering partially to 94.5 Hz after GAN post-training — still far above all baselines (DAC 22.3 Hz). What causes the severe pitch degradation at the auxiliary decoder stage specifically, and why does GAN post-training fail to restore pitch accuracy to pre-auxiliary-decoder levels?

**Q2.** Could the authors report results for REPA-H with adversarial post-training in order to isolate the contribution of the ReGen architecture from the effect of GAN fine-tuning?

**Q3.** What causes the catastrophic failure of ReGen-U at λ_regen = 0.1 (WER 89.94), and is this instability due to gradient interference between shared generation layers or simply the scaling of the regeneration weight?

**Q4.** Could the authors clarify the statement in §7.3 that ReGenVAE outperforms ground-truth samples, given that Table 3 shows improvements only in WER (2.03 vs. 2.14) but not in speaker similarity (0.72 vs. 0.73)?

**Q5.** Have the authors evaluated GFM outside of audio tasks, or considered a simple experiment demonstrating whether velocity-space repulsion improves conditional flow matching in non-audio settings?
reviews.limitations:
The paper includes a Limitations appendix and Social Negative Impact discussion (voice cloning risks, watermarking mitigations, code release with ethical guidelines) above average in transparency. Two additions are recommended for the main paper: 
- explicit acknowledgment of the pitch degradation issue, and 
- a note on the English-dominant training data scope and its implications for multilingual generalization.
reviews.final_justification: The authors' post-rebuttal reply confirms that all rebuttal experiments were conducted under fully matched conditions, which resolves the remaining W2 concern. The pitch metric clarification and 24× compression context for W1 are appreciated and reduce the severity of the concern. However, the stage-by-stage mechanistic explanation for why the auxiliary decoder introduces pitch worsening and why GAN post-training only partially recovers it remains absent. As W2 is now fully resolved and W1 is partially clarified, I maintain my score.

reviews.summary: The authors propose to use the concept of representation alignment (REPA) in a hierarchical way (align speech representations with raw waveform, mel features and features from SSL model) for diffusion models either directly generating waveform or latent diffusion models generating compact VAE waveform representation. Two major tasks - speech reconstruction and text-to-speech synthesis - are considered and the corresponding models trained in the framework of hierarchical REPA achieve good performance in terms of efficiency both at training and inference.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Among the strengths of the paper I can mention the following:
1. The concept of hierarchical representation alignment and generation called ReGen-H is tested across many audio-related tasks such as neural compression, prompted reconstruction and text-to-speech synthesis.
2. The authors plan to open-source the codes, which can be essential for the reproducibility given very complex framework they propose with lots of tricks to obtain the best possible results.
3. The authors developed models operating in a streaming manner which can potentially be helpful in practical applications.
4. There is a very detailed ablation study of many components of their systems in Table 4.
5. Rather good efficiency results, e.g. ReGenVoice is more than 10x faster than CosyVoice2 with comparable synthesis quality as follows from Table 7.

However, some of the above strengths have their corresponding weaknesses which I list below:
1. As for neural compression, Tables 1 and 2 do not allow to conclude that the model the authors propose called ReGenTokenizer allows for the best quality-bitrate tradeoff. Moreover, the fact that its VAE counterpart ReGenVAE shows significantly better results even with twice smaller frame rate suggests that ReGenTokenizer degrades significantly when we introduce VQ layer, thus I have some doubts that the framework itself is the best choice for the discrete speech codec.
2. I do not understand the motivation of including ReGenVAE with continuous latent space into the comparison with codecs with discrete codebooks in Tables 1-2.
3. Prompted reconstruction is a very unusual task and I have some doubts about its practical significance. The authors refer to TaDiCodec paper while introducing this task, and this paper has only 4 citations. So, I guess it would be correct to claim that the task of prompted generation is not well-established in the speech community to estimate generative models. Although I could be mistaken, in which case I ask the authors to correct me.
4. As for speech synthesis, I have very big concerns about the results in Table 6. Seed-TTS zero-shot benchmark has a well-established protocol of evaluation (see https://github.com/BytedanceSpeech/seed-tts-eval). According to the respective papers and my own experience of running the mentioned evaluation code, WERs for CosyVoice2 and F5-TTS are 2.57 and 1.83, while in the present paper WERs for the corresponding models are bigger: 3.09 and 2.00.
5. Although ablation study given in Table 4 is very detailed, it only proves that numerous tricks the authors propose (GFM, adversarial post-training with quite intricate choice of discriminators, hierarchical alignment and generation during model training) lead to better quality after the first 0.5M training steps, but we still do not know what is the performance of all mentioned modifications at convergence.

There are also several other drawbacks, which, together with those mentioned above, make me reject the paper:

6. The paper contains several claims without reference to the literature that are not quite clear to me. E.g. lines 016-020 (why? what does acoustic capacity and semantic capacity mean?), and lines 136-139 (why?).
7. Some parts of the paper lack important details. E.g. text-to-speech models ReGenVoice are not described detailed enough (what is implicit latent aligner? I can see a reference to ZipVoice, but I think this is an important TTS module which requires description rather than just reference to some other paper). Also, how weights of STFT loss are warmed up? (see lines 195-196)? What is the balance between losses corresponding to different discriminators during adversarial post-training? Which layers are aligned with which type of representations? Do you align first, second and third DiT Blocks to waveform, mel and SSL features correspondingly as in Figure 1?
8. In general, the pipeline seems overcomplicated - e.g. in order to match CosyVoice2 in subjective metrics so many tricks are necessary (also, see W5). Furthermore, all components of the proposed framework are known from the previous literature and not novel. 
9.  In Table 7 confidence intervals are very tight. Do you use 95% confidence intervals? How do you compute them? From my experience, confidence intervals for speaker similarity (SMOS) are always larger than for naturalness (MOS) (because humans tend to agree less with each other on this aspect) and are always larger than 0.02 for a reasonable number of assessors and diverse test set.
10. Some minor issues which are, however, numerous enough. In lines 138 - 146 $I$ and $\sigma_{min}$ are not defined. Moreover, since waveforms, mel and SSL features have different dimensionality in general, identity matrices $I$ should be different for different representation types. In lines 182-183 $\lambda_{kl}$ is introduced but never used after. In Table 7 the format of MOS scores is wrong: instead of $3.756\pm 0.02$ it should be something like $3.756\pm 0.198$ (the number of digits after decimal point should be the same). In Table 2 for VAE models the authors put $N_{q}$ equal to 1, while it should be either - (as in Table 1) or $\infty$. Not all of the papers are adequately cited, e.g. TaDiCodec paper is cited as ArXiv preprint while this paper was published on NeurIPS 2025.

If some of the weaknesses (in particular, 4, 6, 7 and 9) are properly addressed by the authors during the rebuttal, and key questions below are answered, I could increase my score.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
1. What is the motivation of using multi-scale sub-band CQT discriminator? I know that CQT transform was designed specifically for music processing, why did you decide to use it in speech generation?
2. What is proportional duration in Table 9? You never mention it in the main text.
3. In subjective evaluation on Amazon Mechanical Turk, did you allow only Master assessors to participate in tests? What is the approximate amount of assessors who were found to cheat (who assigned high scores to white noise)? How exactly did you formulate the tasks for evaluating MOS and SMOS?
reviews.final_justification:
As I wrote in my initial review, the paper definitely has its strong points, including its good efficiency results, and in principle can be helpful for speech community (but only if the authors open-source their codes, otherwise it would be very hard to reproduce their pretty complicated framework). 

On the other hand, the original version had lots of weaknesses as I pointed out in the initial review, and some of them were partially addressed in the rebuttal. Some of them maybe were rather subjective (unusual task of prompted generation, including VAEs with continuous latent space in the comparison with models based on discrete codebooks, overcomplicated pipeline, etc.), and it is quite hard to debate on them, although I still maintain my initial opinion on these topics. At the same time, some of my questions were pretty concrete and, in my opinion, the authors did not properly address them. I still have some doubts about confidence intervals (why they are the same for speaker SMOS and MOS? as I mentioned, from my experience those for SMOS are always larger). The authors also made a strong methodological mistake while presenting WER for CosyVoice2 model - there is an open-source testing protocol, and open-source checkpoint. I ran it many times myself and guarantee that the results are always close to 2.57, although there are some deviations related to probabilistic nature of sampling. For some reason the authors decided not to run tests themselves, but to take the results from another paper (not from the original one describing CosyVoice2), and, as one can see from the rebuttal, they still don't see anything wrong in this.

To conclude, I think that this paper has more weaknesses than strengths, and has some big methodological mistakes not acknowledged by its authors. Therefore I recommend to reject this paper.
